description: Add a first-class Plugin Settings system. Plugins can declare a configSchema (typed fields with labels, descriptions, validation) in their SDK class and package.json. Users provide values in config.json under a new 'settings' map. The server resolves settings at load time — url-type settings derive Chrome match patterns and homepage automatically. Settings flow to the extension and are injected into adapters at runtime. Three user surfaces: CLI interactive (opentabs plugin configure), CLI scripted (opentabs config set setting.*), and side panel UI (ConfigDialog with NeedsSetup badge). The e2e-test plugin gains configSchema fields for testing.

userStories.title: Add config schema types to shared package
userStories.description: Define the ConfigSettingDefinition and ConfigSchema types in platform/shared, add configSchema to PluginOpentabsField and PluginPackageJson, parse it in parsePluginPackageJson, and relax the non-empty urlPatterns requirement when a configSchema with a required url field is present.
acceptanceCriteria: ConfigSettingDefinition type exported from @opentabs-dev/shared with fields: type ('url'|'string'|'number'|'boolean'|'select'), label (string), description (optional string), required (optional boolean), placeholder (optional string), options (optional string[] for select type)
ConfigSchema type exported as Record<string, ConfigSettingDefinition>
PluginOpentabsField includes optional configSchema: ConfigSchema
parsePluginPackageJson parses and validates configSchema from the opentabs field
parsePluginPackageJson allows empty urlPatterns when configSchema contains at least one required url-type field
parsePluginPackageJson rejects empty urlPatterns when no configSchema with required url field exists (existing behavior preserved)
ConfigStatePlugin gains optional configSchema and optional resolvedSettings fields
All existing unit tests continue to pass

userStories.title: Add configSchema field and getConfig utility to plugin SDK
userStories.description: Add the optional configSchema field to OpenTabsPlugin base class and a getConfig() SDK utility that reads from globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig at runtime.
acceptanceCriteria: OpenTabsPlugin has optional readonly configSchema property typed as ConfigSchema (imported from shared)
getConfig(key) function exported from plugin-sdk that reads globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig[key]
getConfig returns string | number | boolean | undefined
getConfig returns undefined gracefully when pluginConfig is not set (e.g., in tests)
ConfigSchema and ConfigSettingDefinition re-exported from plugin-sdk for plugin author convenience
Unit test verifies getConfig reads from globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig

userStories.title: Serialize configSchema in plugin build tool
userStories.description: Update opentabs-plugin build to include configSchema in dist/tools.json manifest and relax the empty urlPatterns validation in the build tool to allow plugins with a configSchema that has a required url field.
acceptanceCriteria: PluginManifestOutput interface gains optional configSchema field
generateManifest includes configSchema from plugin instance when present
configSchema appears in dist/tools.json when the plugin defines one
validatePlugin allows empty urlPatterns when plugin has configSchema with a required url-type field
Build succeeds for a plugin with empty urlPatterns and a configSchema with required url field

userStories.title: Parse settings from config.json and add settings resolution in MCP server
userStories.description: Extend config.ts to parse a 'settings' map from config.json. Add a settings resolver that derives URL patterns from url-type settings. Update LoadedPlugin and RegisteredPlugin to carry configSchema. Thread configSchema and resolved settings through the discovery and registry pipeline.
acceptanceCriteria: OpentabsConfig interface gains settings: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>
loadConfig parses the settings field from config.json
LoadedPlugin gains optional configSchema field
RegisteredPlugin gains optional configSchema field
loadPlugin reads configSchema from tools.json manifest and includes it in LoadedPlugin
During discovery, url-type settings with user-provided values derive Chrome match patterns appended to static urlPatterns
A plugin with empty urlPatterns and a configured instanceUrl gets an effective pattern like *://hostname/*
When no user value is provided for a required url setting, the plugin still loads (with empty effective urlPatterns)
Settings validation: url-type values must parse as valid URLs (new URL() succeeds), invalid values are logged and skipped
saveConfig preserves the settings field when writing config back
Unit tests cover settings parsing, URL pattern derivation, and invalid URL handling

userStories.title: Add settings to server state and config state payload
userStories.description: Store user settings on ServerState, include configSchema and resolvedSettings in the config.getState payload (ConfigStatePlugin), and create a new config.setPluginSettings handler and HTTP endpoint for saving settings.
acceptanceCriteria: ServerState gains pluginSettings field: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>
buildConfigStatePayload includes configSchema and resolvedSettings in each ConfigStatePlugin entry
New handleConfigSetPluginSettings handler processes config.setPluginSettings JSON-RPC requests from the extension
The handler validates settings against the plugin's configSchema (type checking, required fields)
The handler writes to config.json (settings field), triggers reload, and broadcasts plugins.changed
New POST /plugin-settings HTTP endpoint for CLI use (accepts JSON body with plugin name and settings, bearer auth)
Unit tests cover the handler validation and the HTTP endpoint
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/mcp-server/src/state.ts (add pluginSettings to ServerState and createState)
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.ts (add handleConfigSetPluginSettings, update buildConfigStatePayload and serializePluginForExtension)
- platform/mcp-server/src/extension-handlers.test.ts (tests)
- platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.ts (add POST /plugin-settings route)
- platform/mcp-server/src/http-routes.test.ts (tests)
- platform/mcp-server/src/config.ts (add savePluginSettings function)
- platform/mcp-server/src/reload.ts or index.ts (thread settings through reloadCore)

Context:
ServerState at line 268 in state.ts already has pluginPermissions:
```typescript
pluginPermissions: Record<string, PluginPermissionConfig>;
```
Add a similar field:
```typescript
pluginSettings: Record<string, Record<string, unknown>>;
```

createState at line 342:
```typescript
pluginPermissions: {},
```
Add:
```typescript
pluginSettings: {},
```

buildConfigStatePayload at line 342 in extension-handlers.ts builds the ConfigStatePlugin objects. Currently:
```typescript
return {
  ...serializePluginForExtension(state, p),
  source: p.source,
  tabState: tabInfo?.state ?? 'closed',
  // ...
};
```
Add configSchema and resolvedSettings here.

serializePluginForExtension at line 144 returns plugin metadata. Add configSchema from plugin.configSchema and resolvedSettings from state.pluginSettings[plugin.name] resolved against the schema.

The existing handleConfigSetPluginPermission pattern (line 483) is the model for handleConfigSetPluginSettings: validate params, update state, persist to config, broadcast plugins.changed.

For the HTTP endpoint, follow the pattern of POST /reload in http-routes.ts: bearer auth, JSON body, respond with ok.

The savePluginSettings function in config.ts should follow the savePluginPermissions pattern — read-modify-write with the configWriteMutex, only updating the settings field.

Constraints:
- Do NOT mix settings and permissions in the same config map — they are separate concerns.
- The HTTP endpoint must require bearer auth (same secret as /reload).
- Settings validation should be lenient — store the values even if the plugin isn't loaded yet (user may configure before installing).
- When broadcasting plugins.changed after a settings save, the server must reload first so URL patterns are re-derived.
- Bump STATE_SCHEMA_VERSION since ServerState gains a new field.

userStories.title: Inject resolved settings into adapter IIFE at runtime
userStories.description: Update the adapter IIFE wrapper generated by opentabs-plugin build to populate globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig from settings injected at adapter injection time by the Chrome extension background script.
acceptanceCriteria: The adapter IIFE wrapper code sets globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig from an injected settings object
The extension background script passes resolved settings when injecting the adapter via chrome.scripting.executeScript
When no settings exist for a plugin, pluginConfig is set to an empty object (not undefined)
The getConfig SDK utility can read values injected this way
Settings injection does not break existing adapter functionality (tools, lifecycle hooks, logging)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/plugin-tools/src/commands/build.ts (update wrapperCode to read injected config)
- platform/browser-extension/src/background.ts (pass settings when injecting adapter)

Context:
The adapter IIFE wrapper in build.ts (around line 690) sets up globalThis.__openTabs:
```typescript
if (!globalThis.__openTabs) {
  globalThis.__openTabs = {} as OpenTabsRuntime;
}
```

The extension injects adapters via chrome.scripting.executeScript. The server sends plugin metadata (including iife, urlPatterns, etc.) to the extension via sync.full. The extension stores plugins in its local state and injects the IIFE when a matching tab is found.

The injection path is: server sends sync.full with plugin data → extension stores it → when tab matches, extension calls chrome.scripting.executeScript with the IIFE code.

For settings injection, there are two approaches:
A) Include a settings JSON string in the IIFE itself (generated at build time) — but this means the IIFE is static and settings changes require rebuild.
B) The extension injects settings as a separate script before the adapter IIFE — sets globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig = {...}.
C) The adapter IIFE reads from a well-known location that the extension populates.

Approach B is cleanest — the extension injects a tiny settings script before the main IIFE:
```javascript
globalThis.__openTabs = globalThis.__openTabs || {};
globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig = {"instanceUrl":"https://sqlpad.mycompany.com"};
```

Then the adapter IIFE reads it. This way settings changes don't require plugin rebuild — just server reload.

The settings to inject come from the server's state.pluginSettings, resolved and sent to the extension alongside the plugin metadata in sync.full.

Changes:
1. The server's sync.full payload for each plugin should include resolvedSettings (from settings-resolver.ts).
2. The extension, when injecting the adapter, first injects a small config script that sets pluginConfig.
3. The adapter wrapper code does NOT need changes — getConfig already reads from globalThis.__openTabs.pluginConfig.

Alternatively, the wrapper code in build.ts can include a line that initializes pluginConfig from a placeholder that gets replaced. But the separate injection approach is cleaner.

Constraints:
- Settings must be injected BEFORE the adapter IIFE runs (so getConfig works in onActivate and tool handlers).
- The injection must work with the existing CSP-compliant injection model.
- Do NOT embed settings in the IIFE at build time — settings are runtime configuration, not build-time.

userStories.title: Add side panel ConfigDialog component and NeedsSetup badge
userStories.description: Create a ConfigDialog component that dynamically renders form fields from a plugin's configSchema, add a 'Needs Setup' badge to PluginCard for unconfigured plugins, and add a 'Settings' menu item to PluginMenu for configured plugins.
acceptanceCriteria: ConfigDialog renders form fields for each configSchema entry: Input for url/string, number input for number, Switch for boolean, Select for select
ConfigDialog validates url fields (must be valid URL), marks required fields with asterisk, shows placeholder text
ConfigDialog pre-fills current values from resolvedSettings and saves via bg:setPluginSettings
ConfigDialog follows retro theme: border-2, border-border, rounded, shadow-md, font-mono for labels
PluginCard shows a NeedsSetup badge (Settings lucide icon + 'Needs Setup' text) when plugin has configSchema with required unconfigured fields
When expanded, unconfigured plugin shows a 'Configure' button inside the card content area instead of tools
PluginMenu gains a 'Settings' item (Cog icon) visible only when plugin has a configSchema
Both the Configure button and Settings menu item open the ConfigDialog
Storybook stories for ConfigDialog covering: url field, string field, number field, boolean field, select field, validation errors, pre-filled values
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/ConfigDialog.tsx (new file)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/ConfigDialog.stories.tsx (new file)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginCard.tsx (add NeedsSetup badge and Configure button)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/components/PluginMenu.tsx (add Settings menu item)
- platform/browser-extension/src/side-panel/bridge.ts (add setPluginSettings function, update PluginState with configSchema/resolvedSettings)

Context:
PluginCard.tsx renders the plugin card with header (icon, name, chevron, menu, permission select) and content (tool list). The header already has conditional badges:
```typescript
{plugin.source === 'local' && (
  <Badge variant="default" size="sm" className="align-middle">DEV</Badge>
)}
{!plugin.reviewed && (
  <Tooltip>...<ShieldQuestionMark .../></Tooltip>
)}
```
Add the NeedsSetup badge in the same pattern.

PluginMenu.tsx has the kebab menu with VersionItem and remove. Add Settings between them:
```typescript
{plugin.configSchema && Object.keys(plugin.configSchema).length > 0 && (
  <Menu.Item onSelect={() => setConfigOpen(true)}>
    <Settings className="h-3.5 w-3.5" />Settings
  </Menu.Item>
)}
```

For the unconfigured state, when the card content expands:
- If plugin has unconfigured required settings, show a banner instead of tool list
- The banner has text and a Configure button
- Tools are hidden until configuration is complete

The ConfigDialog should use:
- Dialog from retro/Dialog.tsx (already used for unreviewed plugin confirmation)
- Input from retro/Input.tsx
- Select from retro/Select.tsx
- Switch from retro/Switch.tsx
- Button from retro/Button.tsx

For the bridge, add:
```typescript
const setPluginSettings = (plugin: string, settings: Record<string, unknown>): Promise<unknown> =>
  sendBgMessage({ type: 'bg:setPluginSettings', plugin, settings });
```

PluginState (which is Omit<ConfigStatePlugin, 'tools'> & { tools?: ... }) will automatically get configSchema and resolvedSettings from ConfigStatePlugin.

NeedsSetup detection: a plugin needs setup if it has configSchema with at least one required field AND that field is not in resolvedSettings (or resolvedSettings is empty).

Constraints:
- The ConfigDialog MUST follow the retro theme exactly. Use theme-aware Tailwind classes only.
- Use the existing Input, Select, Switch, Dialog, Button retro primitives — do NOT create new UI primitives for this.
- The Settings icon from lucide-react is the correct icon for the NeedsSetup badge (already imported as 'Settings' in other components, or use 'Cog').
- The form should be uncontrolled with commit-on-save (not controlled inputs that fight the user).
- URL validation should use try { new URL(value) } catch — not regex.

userStories.title: Wire bg:setPluginSettings through extension background to MCP server
userStories.description: Add the bg:setPluginSettings message handler in the extension background script that relays settings save requests from the side panel to the MCP server via the WebSocket connection.
acceptanceCriteria: Background script handles bg:setPluginSettings messages from the side panel
The handler sends a config.setPluginSettings JSON-RPC request over the WebSocket to the MCP server
On success, returns { ok: true } to the side panel
On error, propagates the error message back to the side panel
The extension protocol handler (MCP server side) routes config.setPluginSettings to handleConfigSetPluginSettings

userStories.title: Add opentabs plugin configure CLI command
userStories.description: Add the interactive 'opentabs plugin configure <name>' command that reads a plugin's configSchema, prompts for each field, validates inputs, saves to config.json, and notifies the server.
acceptanceCriteria: opentabs plugin configure <name> reads the plugin's configSchema from its installed package (dist/tools.json)
Prompts for each field in order: label, description, placeholder shown as hint, required fields enforced
URL fields validated with new URL() — reprompts on invalid input
Pre-fills current values from config.json settings map when editing existing config
Writes to config.json under the settings map and notifies the server via POST /reload
Prints clear error if plugin is not installed or has no configSchema
Works offline (reads from disk, server notification is best-effort)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- platform/cli/src/commands/plugin.ts (add configure subcommand and handler)
- platform/cli/src/commands/plugin.test.ts (tests)
- platform/cli/src/config.ts (add readPluginConfigSchema and readPluginSettings helpers)

Context:
The plugin command is registered at line 658:
```typescript
const registerPluginCommand = (program: Command): void => {
  const pluginCmd = program.command('plugin').description('Manage plugins')...
```

Existing subcommands: search, list, install, remove, create. Add configure.

The configure handler needs to:
1. Find the plugin on disk (scan global npm + local plugins, similar to readLocalPluginInfo at line 450)
2. Read its dist/tools.json to get configSchema
3. Read current settings from config.json
4. Use readline (Node.js built-in) or a simple prompt library for interactive input
5. Validate and save

For reading tools.json:
```typescript
const toolsJsonPath = join(pluginDir, 'dist', TOOLS_FILENAME);
const manifest = JSON.parse(await readFile(toolsJsonPath, 'utf-8'));
const configSchema = manifest.configSchema;
```

For reading current settings, add to cli/src/config.ts:
```typescript
const getPluginSettings = (config: Record<string, unknown>, pluginName: string): Record<string, unknown> => {
  const settings = config.settings;
  if (!settings || typeof settings !== 'object' || Array.isArray(settings)) return {};
  const pluginSettings = (settings as Record<string, unknown>)[pluginName];
  if (!pluginSettings || typeof pluginSettings !== 'object' || Array.isArray(pluginSettings)) return {};
  return pluginSettings as Record<string, unknown>;
};
```

For the interactive prompt, use Node.js readline/promises:
```typescript
import { createInterface } from 'node:readline/promises';
const rl = createInterface({ input: process.stdin, output: process.stdout });
```

The registration:
```typescript
pluginCmd
  .command('configure')
  .alias('config')
  .description('Configure plugin settings (e.g., instance URL for self-hosted tools)')
  .argument('<name>', 'Plugin name (e.g., sqlpad, github)')
  .addHelpText('after', `\nExamples:\n  $ opentabs plugin configure sqlpad\n  $ opentabs plugin configure github`)
  .action(...);
```

Constraints:
- Use Node.js readline/promises (built-in, no new dependencies).
- Match the existing CLI output style: picocolors for formatting, pc.bold for plugin names, pc.dim for hints.
- The handler must work even if the server is not running (offline mode).
- For select-type fields, show numbered options and accept the number.
- Do NOT prompt for fields that already have values unless the user wants to change them — show current values and let them press Enter to keep.

For each package CLAUDE.md, add documentation in the appropriate section (Key Files, overview, or a new section).

userStories.title: Add E2E tests for plugin settings flow
userStories.description: Write E2E tests verifying the full plugin settings flow: configSchema in health endpoint, settings resolution deriving URL patterns, getConfig reading resolved values, side panel showing configSchema, and CLI config set setting.* working.
acceptanceCriteria: E2E test verifies /health endpoint includes configSchema for e2e-test plugin
E2E test verifies that writing settings to config.json and reloading causes URL pattern derivation for url-type settings
E2E test verifies the sdk_get_config tool returns values configured via settings
E2E test verifies side panel displays configSchema fields for the e2e-test plugin (via plugin card inspection)
E2E test verifies the NeedsSetup badge does NOT appear when all required fields are satisfied
All tests pass in parallel execution (isolated config dirs)
userStories.notes:
Files:
- e2e/plugin-settings.e2e.ts (new file)

Context:
E2E tests use the fixture system in fixtures.ts. Each test gets:
- Its own MCP server on an ephemeral port
- Its own config directory (OPENTABS_CONFIG_DIR)
- Its own Chrome extension + browser context
- Its own MCP client

The e2e-test plugin is registered in localPlugins by the fixture.

Test scenarios:
1. Health endpoint includes configSchema:
```typescript
test('health endpoint includes configSchema', async ({ mcpServer }) => {
  const health = await fetchHealth(mcpServer);
  const plugin = health.pluginDetails.find(p => p.name === 'e2e-test');
  expect(plugin.configSchema).toBeDefined();
  expect(plugin.configSchema.instanceUrl.type).toBe('url');
});
```

2. Settings resolution (URL pattern derivation):
- Write settings to the test's config.json:
```typescript
const config = JSON.parse(await readFile(configPath, 'utf-8'));
config.settings = { 'e2e-test': { instanceUrl: 'http://localhost:9999' } };
await writeFile(configPath, JSON.stringify(config, null, 2));
```
- Trigger reload (POST /reload)
- Verify /health shows the derived URL pattern in the plugin's urlPatterns

3. getConfig tool:
- Configure settings in config.json
- Reload
- Call sdk_get_config tool via MCP client
- Verify it returns the configured value

4. Side panel configSchema display:
- Open side panel
- Find e2e-test plugin card
- Verify it shows the Settings menu item in the kebab menu
- Since e2e-test has no required unconfigured fields (instanceUrl is optional), NeedsSetup badge should NOT appear

Follow the patterns in existing E2E tests:
- Import { test, expect } from './fixtures.js'
- Use mcpServer, testServer, extensionContext, mcpClient fixtures
- Use helpers from helpers.ts (openSidePanel, getExtensionId, etc.)

Constraints:
- Tests must be isolated (each gets its own config dir and server)
- Tests must pass in parallel execution
- Use the existing fixture system — do NOT create custom server setup
- Test file should be named plugin-settings.e2e.ts following existing convention
